items.q: What are the main Christian theories of atonement?
items.correct: Penal substitution (Christ bore the punishment for sin), Christus Victor (Christ defeated evil powers through the cross), ransom theory (Christ paid a price to free humanity), and moral exemplar (Christ's death inspires love). Different traditions emphasise different theories.
items.misconception: All Christians believe Jesus died only to pay God back for sin.
items.random_wrong: Atonement means Jesus apologised to humans for being born divine.
items.why: Penal substitution dominates Protestant evangelicalism but isn't the only or oldest model. Christus Victor was prevalent in the early church and Orthodox traditions still emphasise it. Students often treat one model as 'the' Christian view of atonement.

items.q: What is the problem of evil?
items.correct: If God is all-loving, all-knowing and all-powerful, why does evil and suffering exist? Logically, all four cannot be true together without explanation.
items.misconception: It is the question of whether evil people will go to hell.
items.random_wrong: It is the question of whether the devil really exists.
items.why: Students treat it as a moral or eschatological question. It is actually a logical challenge to classical theism — and the central problem theodicies try to solve.

items.q: What is the difference between act and rule utilitarianism?
items.correct: Act utilitarianism judges each individual action by its consequences; rule utilitarianism follows rules that, if generally adopted, would produce the most good — sacrificing case-by-case calculation for predictability and fairness.
items.misconception: Act and rule utilitarianism are basically the same approach.
items.random_wrong: Act utilitarianism is about action movies and rule utilitarianism is about laws.
items.why: The distinction matters for famous counterexamples (e.g. punishing the innocent). Act utilitarianism might endorse it if outcomes are better; rule utilitarianism rejects such practices because the rule would be harmful overall.

items.q: In which city was Muhammad born?
items.why: Correct answer: Mecca. “Medina” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: One who submits to God. “One who fasts” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In which year (CE) did Muhammad die?
items.why: The real figure is 632. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
